found.angle: Rhythm quantization and tuplet detection for performance-MIDI-to-score. The literature converges on one root cause for tuplet UNDER-production and MIS-placement: a flat absolute time grid (our 1/24 quarter grid) forces the model to infer ternary-vs-binary subdivision from raw timing, which is exactly the ambiguity expressive timing destroys. The field's fixes are structural, not loss-reweighting: (a) make the grid BEAT-RELATIVE so each beat carries its own subdivision decision; (b) model note value as P(note_value | metrical_position) so triplet durations are only probable at triplet-grid positions; (c) enforce well-formed-tuplet constraints at decode via a grammar/automaton so the model can never emit a 'lone' or incomplete triplet (the failure mode behind bad placement); (d) use non-local/self-similarity priors so a tuplet decision propagates to repeated material instead of firing on the wrong piece. These attack PLACEMENT specifically, which global count knobs cannot.

findings.technique: Beat-relative (per-beat sub-tatum) onset grid instead of an absolute 1/24-quarter grid: interpolate detected/annotated beats into a fixed number of sub-beats per beat (e.g. 12 sub-beats = a 32nd-note-triplet grid) and express every onset/duration RELATIVE to its enclosing beat. This converts the global 'is this a triplet?' question into a local per-beat subdivision choice, which is where tuplet placement actually lives.
findings.source: Wachter, Murgul & Heizmann, 'Beat-Based Rhythm Quantization of MIDI Performances', AES AIML-Audio 2025 (arXiv:2508.19262). T5 transformer; interpolates beats to 12 equidistant ticks/beat (32nd-triplet grid); onsets/durations quantized beat-relative via Euclidean distance. Beats MUSTER e_onset 12.30 vs MIDI2ScoreTransformer 15.55, HMM-classical 22.58. Explicitly notes higher e_offset stems from 'inability to detect 32nd notes' = subdivision-depth limit.
findings.how_to_apply: Re-tokenize the encoder/decoder so onsets are (beat_index, sub_beat_in_0..23) rather than absolute 1/24-quarter positions; keep 24 sub-beats so straight (multiples of 3) and triplet (multiples of 8) and sextuplet positions stay representable. Feed ASAP beat annotations (you already have them) as input during training and a beat-tracker at inference. This directly removes the global-grid ambiguity that makes the model default to 'no tuplet': within a beat, a triplet is now just sub-beat in {0,8,16} vs straight {0,6,12,18}. This is the single highest-leverage change for placement and is architecture-compatible with your RoFormer (just a tokenizer + an extra beat-position input embedding).

findings.technique: Two-stage neural beat tracking then beat-conditioned quantization (CRNN predicts beats/downbeats; a second model quantizes on-beat AND off-beat notes to a sub-beat grid, jointly with time-sig/key/hand). Decoupling beat estimation from subdivision means tuplets are inferred against a stable local reference frame rather than against expressive absolute time.
findings.source: Liu, Kong, Morfi & Benetos, 'Performance MIDI-to-Score Conversion by Neural Beat Tracking', ISMIR 2022 best paper (archives.ismir.net/ismir2022/paper/000047.pdf; code: github.com/cheriell/PM2S). CRNN predicts whether each note is on-beat, then extended to quantize all beat and non-beat notes plus time/key sig and hand part.
findings.how_to_apply: Your masked-SSL already teaches score structure; add an auxiliary beat/downbeat head on the encoder (multitask) so the model has an explicit metrical frame, then condition the duration/onset decoder on it. Note: your own memory records a beat-conditioning fine-tune was neutral-to-destructive when bolted onto the released ckpt; the lesson from PM2S+Wachter is that beat info must reshape the TOKENIZATION (beat-relative targets) at pretraining time, not be appended as a conditioning token to an absolute-grid model. Retrain the SSL stage with beat-relative targets, not fine-tune. Use PM2S as a ready beat-tracker to supply beats on unlabeled classical SSL data so beat-conditioning is available for the unpaired corpus too.

findings.technique: Model note value as P(note_value | metrical_position) on a tatum grid whose unit is 1/3 of a 16th note, so triplet/duplet durations are only probable at triplet/duplet grid positions. The 'log note-value probability L_NV' is conditioned on the note's metrical position b_n, plus a 'negative rate of ties across a barline' term that suppresses ill-formed durations.
findings.source: Shibata, Nakamura & Yoshii, 'Non-Local Musical Statistics as Guides for Audio-to-Score Piano Transcription', Information Sciences 566 (2021), arXiv:2008.12710. Metrical HMM with tatum = 1/3 of a 16th note (natively encodes triplets), bar length B in tatums (B=48 for 4/4); L_NV = sum ln P(r_n | b_n) with r_n note value, b_n metrical position; R_tie penalizes ties across barlines.
findings.how_to_apply: Add a duration head whose logits are biased by metrical position: precompute an empirical P(note_value | sub_beat_position) table from your engraved-score corpus and add log P as a prior to the decoder's NoteDuration logits (a position-conditioned logit bias, not a flat tuplet upweight). This makes a triplet duration cheap at sub-beats {0,8,16} and expensive at {6,12,18}, so the model stops scattering tuplets onto wrong (binary) positions — fixing PLACEMENT where your bucket-reweighting only fixed COUNT. Cheap to add: it is a lookup-table logit offset, fully compatible with the RoFormer decoder and your 1/24 grid (map sub-beats to b_n).

findings.technique: Grammar/weighted-tree-automaton decoding that enforces WELL-FORMED tuplets: rhythm is a recursive subdivision tree (a beat splits into binary OR ternary children), rule weights make triplets costlier than duplets, and the 1-best parse guarantees no incomplete/illegal tuplet can be emitted. This is the structural cure for 'tuplet on the wrong piece' and 'lone triplet' artifacts.
findings.source: Foscarin, Jacquemard, Rigaux & Sakai, 'A Parse-Based Framework for Coupled Rhythm Quantization and Score Structuring', MCM 2019 (inria.hal.science/hal-01988990); qparse docs (qparse.gitlabpages.inria.fr/docs/scientific). WCFG/WTA: rule q0->q1 q2 (duplet, weight 6) vs q0->q1 q2 q2 (triplet, weight 12); 'value smaller for a duplet than for a triplet'; 1-best via Knuth/Dijkstra on weighted hypergraph; tuplet completeness guaranteed by grammar, not post-processing.
findings.how_to_apply: Use as a constrained-decoding / reranker layer on top of your RoFormer. Define a small CFG over each beat's 24 sub-tatums (binary chain: 24->12->6->3; ternary: 24->8->4 etc.); at decode, restrict the duration decoder to grammar-legal continuations within the current beat (grammar-masked beam search), or rerank the model's top-k beat-level rhythms by model_logprob + lambda*grammar_weight. Crucially this PREVENTS the model from placing a single triplet member without completing the triplet — the exact placement failure mode. The grammar's per-rule weights also let you tune triplet propensity globally without retraining. Pairs cleanly with the metrical-position prior (technique 3): grammar enforces legality, prior shapes likelihood.

findings.technique: Non-local / self-similarity priors so a tuplet decision propagates to REPEATED material. Use the score self-similarity matrix and global note-value-distribution statistics as guides, so once a passage is read as triplets, its repeats are too — preventing the same figure being binary in one bar and triplet in another (a placement-consistency failure).
findings.source: Shibata, Nakamura & Yoshii 2021 (arXiv:2008.12710), Sec.4: beat/bar-level self-similarity matrix D(Xi,Xj) over pitch sets and note-value sets NV(Xi); auto-similarity indices A4/A3 for 4- vs 3-based meter; uses note-value-set overlap D_r so repeats share rhythmic notation. Same family as Nakamura's repetition-aware grammar (EUSIPCO 2016).
findings.how_to_apply: Two options. (a) Decode-time: after a first pass, compute a note-value self-similarity over beats and run a consistency rerank that rewards assigning the same subdivision to highly-similar (repeated) beats — turns one correct tuplet read into many correctly-placed ones. (b) Train-time: add a contrastive/auxiliary objective in the masked-SSL stage that pushes the encoder to give repeated segments matching rhythm tokens. This targets the 'right rhythm, wrong piece/bar' symptom your global knobs created, by enforcing within-piece rhythmic self-consistency rather than corpus-level rates.

findings.technique: Graph-decoder over candidate notes where edges encode rhythmic grouping, so tuplet membership is predicted jointly across the notes that form a tuplet rather than per-token. Joint group-level prediction is structurally better at deciding WHICH notes co-form a tuplet (placement) than left-to-right token emission.
findings.source: EngravingGNN: 'A Hybrid Graph Neural Network for End-to-End Piano Score Engraving', 2025 (arXiv:2509.19412); benchmarks against MIDI2ScoreTransformer and HMM baselines on MUSTER; nodes=notes, edges=rhythmic/metric relations to propagate timing and learn tuplet groupings. Related: Foscarin et al., 'Predicting Music Hierarchies with a Graph-Based Neural Decoder', 2023 (arXiv:2306.16955).
findings.how_to_apply: Heavier lift, but a targeted version is cheap: add a lightweight 'tuplet-grouping' head on top of your RoFormer decoder hidden states that predicts, for each beat, a pairwise/groupwise 'these k notes form one tuplet' label, trained on engraved-score tuplet brackets (music21 exposes tuplet objects). Use its output to gate the grammar mask (technique 4) or as an auxiliary SSL loss. This makes tuplet placement an explicit grouping decision over co-located notes instead of an emergent property of independent duration tokens — addressing the 0-tuplets-on-9/14-pieces symptom by giving the model a dedicated signal for 'this beat is subdivided ternary'. Skeptical caveat: only worth the engineering if techniques 1+3+4 (beat-relative grid + metrical prior + grammar decode) do not already close the NoteDuration gap; try those first as they reuse your existing architecture.

findings.technique: Output-side beat-relative onset encoding: predict onset as (beat-index, sub-beat-tick) instead of (measure-offset on absolute 1/24 grid), so tuplets become a small categorical choice WITHIN a beat rather than a position that must be inferred globally. Wachter et al. show this beat-relative tokenization beats Beyer's absolute-grid encoding on MUSTER onset error (12.30 vs ~12.69) and generalizes to unseen time signatures with NO time-signature tokens, because rhythm is modeled as deviation from a metrical grid.
findings.source: Wachter et al., 'Beat-Based Rhythm Quantization of MIDI Performances', 2025, arXiv:2508.19262 (also Klangio/Wachter arXiv:2604.22290); builds on Beyer & Dai 2024 arXiv:2410.00210
findings.how_to_apply: Your tokenizer currently encodes onset as measure-relative 'offset' on an absolute 1/24 grid (tokenizer.py PARAMS['offset'], step 1/24, plus the 'downbeat'/measure-length stream). That makes a triplet at offset 1.333 a rare absolute bucket the model rarely commits to, so it defaults to straight grid -> tuplet under-production. Refactor the OUTPUT heads (not just input features): replace/augment the 'offset' head with two heads — a beat-index head and a within-beat subdivision head over a small canonical set {1,2,3,4,6,8} (i.e. which n-tuplet this beat is divided into) plus position-in-subdivision. This makes 'is this beat a triplet?' a single low-cardinality decision the cross-entropy loss can actually learn, and ties duration to it. Train on ASAP ground-truth beats (you already extract performance_beats/downbeats in beat_features.py). Critically this is an output-representation change — your already-tested INPUT beat-phase features (beat_features.py) were neutral-to-destructive precisely because conditioning the encoder does not change what the decoder is asked to emit.

findings.technique: Explicit per-beat tuplet/subdivision tokens with a hierarchical (duple-vs-triple) decision, rather than relying on the fine 1/24 grid to represent tuplets implicitly. Score Transformer divides each beat into 24 timesteps so triplets/32nd-triplets are representable, but reports NO tuplet-specific accuracy and uses implicit duration tokens; Foscarin's grammar instead makes the duple-vs-triple subdivision an explicit, scored decision per beat.
findings.source: Suzuki, 'Score Transformer', ACM MMAsia 2021, arXiv:2112.00355 (24-timesteps/beat, implicit tuplets); Foscarin et al., 'A Parse-Based Framework for Coupled Rhythm Quantization and Score Structuring', 2019, inria.hal.science/hal-01988990 / qparse
findings.how_to_apply: Representability (your 1/24 grid) is not the bottleneck — Score Transformer has the same 24-grid and still has no tuplet guarantee. The fix is to make tuplet membership EXPLICIT so duration tokens are consistent within a tuplet group. Add a small per-note categorical 'tuplet' head to your existing multi-head output (alongside duration/offset/downbeat/grace/trill in tokenizer.py) with classes {none, triplet, sextuplet, quintuplet, ...}. At decode time, enforce group consistency: once a note in a beat is tagged triplet, snap its neighbors' durations to the /3 grid. This converts placement from per-note noise into a per-beat decision and directly attacks the NoteDuration MUSTER sub-stream where your error concentrates.

findings.technique: Grammar / complexity-vs-fitness rescoring of the rhythm output: enumerate candidate beat subdivisions (2,3,4,6...) and pick the one minimizing quantization error PLUS a notation-complexity penalty, so a tuplet is only emitted when it genuinely fits the timing better than the straight grid. This is a global, per-beat consistency prior that a per-note seq2seq decoder lacks.
findings.source: Foscarin et al. 2019, 'A Parse-Based Framework for Coupled Rhythm Quantization and Score Structuring', inria.hal.science/hal-01988990 (qparse weighted context-free grammar); Nakamura et al., 'Non-Local Musical Statistics as Guides for Audio-to-Score Piano Transcription', 2020, arXiv:2008.12710
findings.how_to_apply: Use as a decode-time rescorer over your existing duration/offset logits (no retrain needed first). For each beat, take the model's softmax over duration tokens for the notes in that beat, and for each candidate subdivision n in {2,3,4,6} compute fitness = sum of log-probs of the n-grid-snapped durations minus lambda*complexity(n) (complexity higher for triplets/odd tuplets). Choose argmax and snap. This directly fixes the 'global knobs recover count but not placement' failure: it is per-beat and timing-driven, so it adds triplets only on beats whose performed onsets actually cluster at 0.33/0.67 phase (which beat_features.py already shows happens on Mazeppa) and leaves straight beats alone. Tune lambda on the 14-piece ASAP set against MUSTER. This is the most direct, low-risk lever for placement.

findings.technique: Beat/downbeat tracking as an explicit upstream stage whose predictions ANCHOR the rhythm grid at inference (not just as a soft input feature). A dedicated MIDI beat/downbeat tracker reaches 98% beat / 77% downbeat F1; feeding its hard beat times as the quantization grid removes the model's need to infer the grid itself.
findings.source: 'Beat and Downbeat Tracking in Performance MIDI Using an End-to-End Transformer', 2025, arXiv:2507.00466; Liu et al. PM2S, 'Performance MIDI-to-score conversion by neural beat tracking', ISMIR 2022, archives.ismir.net/ismir2022/paper/000047.pdf (github.com/cheriell/PM2S)
findings.how_to_apply: Your beat_features.py already pulls ASAP ground-truth beats for training but falls back to pretty_midi at inference. The skeptical lesson from your own logs (beat-conditioning memory: neutral-to-destructive, flat gold-beat ceiling) is that good beats as INPUT features did not help. So do not re-run that. Instead use PM2S/2507.00466 beats to DEFINE the grid for the Foscarin-style rescorer above: the rescorer's per-beat subdivision search needs beat boundaries, and using a strong tracker (PM2S onset-F1 ~97%) rather than pretty_midi will sharpen the phase clustering that distinguishes triplet beats from straight beats. Net: beats help at the rescoring/grid stage, not as decoder conditioning.

findings.technique: Note-value-aware data construction & augmentation that preserves tuplet phase: beat-level (not note-level) pair alignment and tempo/onset jitter, so the model sees consistent sub-beat phase for tuplets across performances. Beyer's ablation shows augmentation is 'crucial', and beat-level alignment avoids the one-to-one note assumption that breaks on tuplet-dense passages.
findings.source: Beyer & Dai, 'End-to-end Piano Performance-MIDI to Score Conversion with Transformers', ISMIR 2024, arXiv:2410.00210 (1/24 grid, beat-level greedy alignment, tempo 0.8-1.2 / duration 0.95-1.05 / onset Gaussian sd 0.05 augmentation)
findings.how_to_apply: Two concrete adjustments. (1) In your masked-SSL on unpaired engraved scores, the encoder gets masked timing — make sure the SSL target retains the score's tuplet phase (durations on the /3 grid) and OVERSAMPLE measures containing tuplets at the measure level so the decoder sees tuplet-duration tokens in-context, not just globally reweighted (your global tuplet-bucket reweighting moved count not placement because it was global, not contextual). (2) Keep tempo/onset jitter small (Beyer's ranges) — overly aggressive tempo scaling blurs the 0.33/0.67 phase cue and was shown to HURT downbeat accuracy in arXiv:2507.00466, which would worsen tuplet detection. Pair these with per-head decoding (your model.py already supports head_overrides): use a lower temperature / higher top-k specifically on the duration+tuplet heads so the decoder commits to tuplets it would otherwise smear.

findings.technique: Logit adjustment (post-hoc + loss-based, Fisher-consistent prior correction). Post-hoc: argmax_y [f_y(x) - tau*log(pi_y)]; train-time loss: log[1 + sum_{y'!=y} (pi_{y'}/pi_y)^tau * exp(f_{y'}(x) - f_y(x))], where pi_y is the marginal token prior and tau~1. This is the principled alternative to the loss reweighting you already tried.
findings.source: Menon et al., 'Long-tail learning via logit adjustment', ICLR 2021. https://arxiv.org/abs/2007.07314
findings.how_to_apply: Your failed 'tuplet-bucket loss reweighting' multiplies the loss by 1/pi; this paper proves that is NOT Fisher-consistent in separable regimes (it 'has minimal effect in separable settings') and tends to over-emit. Logit adjustment instead shifts the DECISION BOUNDARY by an additive offset on the logit of each duration token, which is exactly the lever for placement: it only flips a NoteDuration prediction to a tuplet value when the model's evidence already nearly supports it, rather than globally inflating tuplet probability. CONCRETE: estimate pi over your 1/24-grid NoteDuration vocabulary (per-token, e.g. duration=1/3, 1/6, 1/12 quarter are the tuplet buckets) from the training corpus; add tau*log(pi_y) inside the decoder's duration softmax during fine-tuning. Critically, apply it ONLY to the NoteDuration sub-vocabulary (the stream where your error concentrates), not pitch/voice heads. Start with the post-hoc version first (zero retraining): sweep tau in [0.5,1.5] at inference on the duration logits and measure MUSTER NoteDuration ER on the 14-piece set — this directly tests whether the count/placement tradeoff is a calibration problem before you spend a fine-tune. Because the offset is per-token and additive, pieces with no triplet evidence keep low triplet logits (placement preserved), unlike resampling which forced triplets onto wrong pieces.

findings.technique: Token-level adaptive training with a floored, expectation-bounded frequency weight. Weight w(y)=A*exp(-T*Count(y))+1 (or chi-square form A*Count(y)^2*exp(-T*Count(y))+1 for noisy/very-rare tokens), with two hard constraints: every weight >= 1, and the vocabulary-weighted mean stays in [1, 1+delta].
findings.source: Gu, Zhang et al., 'Token-level Adaptive Training for Neural Machine Translation', EMNLP 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.04380
findings.how_to_apply: This is the corrected version of the focal-loss/linear-reweight family that the authors show 'does much harm to high-frequency tokens.' The min-weight>=1 floor and the expectation bound [1,1+delta] are the missing guardrails on your bucket reweighting — they up-weight tuplet durations WITHOUT down-weighting the common dotted/binary durations that dominate the 9 pieces where you currently emit zero tuplets, so you stop trading away NoteDuration accuracy on non-tuplet pieces. CONCRETE: compute Count(y) over NoteDuration tokens on the 1/24 grid; the tuplet buckets (1/3,1/6,1/12,5/24 etc.) get the largest w because they're rarest. Use the chi-square form because your tuplet tokens are extreme-tail (it dampens the very rarest so a single mislabeled 1/24 token doesn't dominate). Apply as a SHORT low-LR fine-tune on top of your existing masked-SSL+ASAP checkpoint (their recipe is 'train normally, then fine-tune with the adaptive objective at low LR'), which matches your need for a non-drifting vehicle (per data-scaling-derisk memory). Tune T so the floor mostly bites only on the tuplet tail; verify the weighted-mean bound delta<=~0.3 to bound MUSTER drift on the other sub-streams.

findings.technique: Difficulty-based (entropy) loss reweighting that respects multiple valid continuations: L = -(1 - H(p))^gamma * log(p_target), where H(p)=-sum_j p_j log p_j is the entropy of the model's full predictive distribution at that step and gamma controls strength.
findings.source: Su et al., 'MiLe Loss: A New Loss for Mitigating the Bias of Learning Difficulties in Generative Language Models', NAACL Findings 2024. https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.19531
findings.how_to_apply: Directly targets why naive focal loss is the wrong tool for YOUR problem. Focal loss keys off only the gold token's probability, so at a genuinely ambiguous rhythmic position (a quarter that could quantize to binary or to a triplet) it mislabels the step as 'hard' and over-amplifies — this is plausibly how your global knobs pushed tuplets onto wrong pieces. MiLe instead weights by the ENTROPY of the whole duration distribution, so it up-weights steps where the model is genuinely confused across duration options (the true tuplet-vs-binary decision points) and leaves confident binary-rhythm steps alone. CONCRETE: apply the (1-H(p))^gamma factor only to the NoteDuration head's per-step CE, computing H over the duration sub-vocabulary on the 1/24 grid. This is a drop-in multiplier needing no frequency statistics and no resampling, so it cannot reweight the corpus toward wrong pieces — it is purely local/context-dependent, which is the property your count-recovering-but-placement-breaking knobs lacked. Sweep gamma in [0.5,2]; watch that overall NoteDuration ER and the count-vs-placement balance on the 14 pieces. Skeptical note: the paper reports overall perplexity can rise slightly while medium/difficult-token perplexity drops — acceptable here since MUSTER NoteDuration ER is your target, not global likelihood.

findings.technique: Fix rare-token representation anisotropy via adaptive gradient gating (AGG) and/or frequency-aware contrastive learning, so rare duration embeddings are not squashed into a narrow cone with small norms.
findings.source: Yu et al., 'Rare Tokens Degenerate All Tokens: Improving Neural Text Generation via Adaptive Gradient Gating for Rare Token Embeddings', ACL 2022 (https://aclanthology.org/2022.acl-long.3/); Zhang et al., 'Frequency-Aware Contrastive Learning for Neural Machine Translation', AAAI 2022 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.14484).
findings.how_to_apply: This addresses PLACEMENT at the representation level, which loss reweighting cannot. The diagnosis: rare tokens receive a persistent gradient component that pulls their embeddings together (anisotropic 'narrow cone'), making the decoder unable to discriminate WHEN a triplet duration applies vs a binary one — consistent with your symptom that forcing emission lands triplets on the wrong pieces (the model can produce the token but cannot place it because the rare-duration embeddings are not separable). CONCRETE: (a) AGG — gate the common 'pull-together' part of the gradient on the rare NoteDuration output embeddings (1/3,1/6,1/12,...) so they keep larger, better-separated norms; this is a training-time gradient hook on the duration output projection, no loss change. (b) FCL — add a contrastive term that pushes apart representations of low-frequency duration tokens, weighted by frequency. Both are most useful BEFORE/alongside the logit/loss fixes above: calibration fixes the boundary, but only separable rare-duration representations let the decoder put the boundary in the right CONTEXT. Skeptical note: these were validated on word embeddings/NMT, not rhythm grids; treat as a representation-quality experiment to run if logit-adjustment+MiLe recover count but still misplace.

findings.technique: Decouple 'is there a tuplet here?' (placement/detection) from 'which duration token' (emission) — a two-stage / gated formulation that lets you maximize placement precision separately from the rare-token softmax, analogous to objectness-then-class decoupling in two-stage detectors and copy/switch gating in seq2seq.
findings.source: Two-stage detection probabilistic decoupling overview (https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/two-stage-detection-framework); Gu et al. CopyNet copy/switch gating, ACL 2016 (https://www.researchgate.net/publication/306093800_Incorporating_Copying_Mechanism_in_Sequence-to-Sequence_Learning).
findings.how_to_apply: Your core failure is that COUNT and PLACEMENT are entangled in one duration softmax, so global knobs move count without controlling placement. Add an auxiliary per-note binary head 'is-tuplet-member' (predicting whether this note's duration lies off the binary grid, i.e. denominator divisible by 3 on the 1/24 grid) trained with its own (class-balanced or logit-adjusted) loss. At decode, gate the tuplet-valued duration tokens by this head's calibrated probability: only allow 1/3,1/6,1/12,... when the detector fires above a threshold tuned for MUSTER NoteDuration. This converts the problem into a calibrated detection threshold you can sweep per the precision/recall tradeoff you actually care about, instead of an all-or-nothing softmax bias. The detector can also be conditioned on encoder features (the masked-SSL encoder sees real onset micro-timing in the performance MIDI), giving it genuine per-piece evidence for placement — the signal your global resampling threw away. Skeptical note: this is an architectural add (one head + a gate), so it costs a retrain; justify it only if the cheaper logit-adjustment/MiLe loss experiments plateau on placement.

findings.technique: Conditioning instead of (or before) reweighting: prefer per-step, context-dependent signals over global corpus-level steering; if you must steer globally, condition on an explicit input flag rather than reweighting the loss/data.
findings.source: Synthesis grounded in the reweighting-vs-conditioning contrast across the above sources, esp. logit adjustment's swap of train-prior for desired test-prior (Menon et al. 2021, https://arxiv.org/abs/2007.07314) and token-level (local) vs sequence-level (global) reweighting (Gu et al. 2020, https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.04380).
findings.how_to_apply: Explains mechanistically WHY your two failed knobs failed: tuplet-bucket loss reweighting and tuplet-rate corpus resampling are both GLOBAL, sequence/corpus-level priors, so they shift the marginal tuplet rate uniformly and cannot respect that tuplets belong on specific pieces/passages — they raise count and necessarily misplace. Every high-relevance technique above replaces a global prior with a LOCAL signal: logit adjustment is per-token-class (still global but boundary-correct, not mass-inflating), MiLe is per-step entropy (fully local), the detector head is per-note (fully contextual). PRACTICAL COROLLARY from logit adjustment's train-prior/test-prior swap: if you want a global tuplet-rate dial for product use, expose it as an INFERENCE-time tau on the duration logits (swap the training prior for a target prior at decode) rather than baking it into training data — this keeps placement intact on no-tuplet pieces and is reversible per piece. Bottom line for sequencing the experiments: try post-hoc logit-adjustment tau-sweep (no retrain) -> MiLe duration-head fine-tune -> token-adaptive floored weights -> only then the detector head / anisotropy fixes.

findings.technique: Beat/downbeat-grid pre-quantization as an explicit conditioning signal (encode the metrical grid, not just raw timing). Annotated beats are interpolated to 12 equidistant sub-beat 'ticks' per beat — exactly a 32nd-note-triplet grid — and every performance onset/duration is snapped to that grid before/while feeding the model. This is THE most relevant result: same ASAP dataset, same MUSTER metric, same 1/24-style triplet-capable grid as us.
findings.source: Transformer-Based Rhythm Quantization of Performance MIDI Using Beat Annotations, 2026 (arXiv:2604.22290). https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22290
findings.how_to_apply: Our model currently infers metrical position end-to-end from raw onset/duration buckets; tuplet PLACEMENT is precisely a 'where is the beat grid' problem. ASAP ships per-beat and per-downbeat annotations — feed them. Concretely: (1) compute beat times (from ASAP gold annotations for eval, or a beat-tracker like the companion arXiv:2507.00466 transformer beat-tracker at train/inference for unpaired data), (2) for each input MIDI note add two conditioning channels — its fractional phase within the current beat (onset_time - beat_start)/(beat_end - beat_start) in [0,1), and its inter-beat tick index quantized to 12 — as extra embedded input tokens/features alongside pitch/onset/dur/velocity. Because 12 ticks resolves 16th-triplets and our output is 1/24, a note whose beat-phase lands near 1/3 or 2/3 is mechanically a triplet; giving the encoder the phase removes the need to reconstruct it from absolute timing. This targets the NoteDuration sub-stream directly and fixes placement (right pieces) because the grid is per-note, not a global rate. Their note-value accuracy 83.3% with beats vs the end-to-end SOTA's lower MUSTER NoteDuration is the ablation evidence. Start by adding beat-phase as an input feature to the existing RoFormer encoder; no architecture change needed.

findings.technique: Per-bar 'expert-feature' description conditioning via description-to-sequence (FIGARO). Hand-crafted, human-interpretable features (incl. note density) are computed per bar, quantized to a special vocabulary, concatenated into a description sequence, and fed to the encoder; the decoder reconstructs the music conditioned on it. The masked-SSL reconstruction setup is structurally identical to ours.
findings.source: FIGARO: Controllable Music Generation using Learned and Expert Features, ICLR 2023 (arXiv:2201.10936). https://arxiv.org/html/2201.10936v4
findings.how_to_apply: Add a LOCAL tuplet-presence control rather than the global knobs that failed. During masked-SSL on engraved scores you have the ground-truth score, so per bar (or per beat) compute a cheap binary/categorical 'tuplet-present' feature (= does this bar contain any note whose duration denominator is not a power of 2, i.e. uses the /3 part of the 1/24 grid) plus a note-density bucket. Prepend or interleave these as conditioning tokens aligned to bar boundaries in the encoder input. Crucially this is auto-derivable from the target during SSL, so it's free supervision; at paired-data train time derive it from the score side too. This directly fixes PLACEMENT: the failed global reweighting/resampling raised tuplet COUNT but couldn't say WHICH bars — a per-bar tuplet-presence channel tells the decoder exactly which bars should carry tuplets. At inference you don't have the target, so either (a) train a tiny auxiliary head/classifier on the encoder states to PREDICT per-bar tuplet-presence from the performance MIDI (the beat-phase distribution within a bar is highly diagnostic of triplets), then feed its prediction back as the control (self-conditioning), or (b) use it teacher-forced for analysis/oracle ceiling to confirm the lever before investing.

findings.technique: Prepended difficulty/rhythmic-density token + auxiliary classification head with gradient detachment. Difficulty is a prepended categorical label; a classification head predicts the target attribute from hidden states (gradient-detached from the prompt tokens) with an auxiliary loss (beta=0.1). Conditioning alone gave 69% control accuracy; adding the auxiliary head raised it to ~93%.
findings.source: Difficulty-Aware Score Generation for Piano Sight-Reading, 2025 (arXiv:2509.16913). https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.16913
findings.how_to_apply: This is the fix for WHY our global reweighting forced tuplets onto the wrong pieces: a single conditioning signal with no auxiliary supervision is weak (their 69% number). Apply two things. (1) Replace/augment the failed corpus-resampling with a per-piece (or per-segment) prepended rhythmic-density token derived from features they list — average IOI, displacement rate — which our performance MIDI gives directly; this conditions the model on observed density instead of forcing a global rate. (2) Add an auxiliary head on the encoder (or decoder) hidden states that predicts per-piece/per-bar tuplet-rate or density, detached so the model can't cheat off the input token, weighted ~0.1. The auxiliary loss forces internal representations to actually encode rhythmic density, which is what makes the conditioning steer PLACEMENT rather than just COUNT. Use their feats vs feats_cot finding: numeric descriptors (IOI/displacement) worked, so feed scalars, not just an 'easy/hard' label.

findings.technique: Self-conditioning on inter-onset-interval (IOI)/timing statistics as explicit input features (rhythm models matter more than acoustic features for transcription). Recognizing rhythm requires looking at IOI structure across time, and explicit rhythm/score-model conditioning consistently improves transcription; multiple MIDI->score transformers tokenize IOI as a first-class input feature.
findings.source: End-to-End Piano Performance-MIDI to Score Conversion with Transformers, 2024 (arXiv:2410.00210, the 1/24-grid SOTA lineage, MUSTER 11.30) + Bayesian piece-specific rhythm-transcription models (arXiv:1908.06969). https://arxiv.org/html/2410.00210v1 ; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1908.06969
findings.how_to_apply: The SOTA baseline feeds only pitch/onset/duration/velocity and infers rhythm end-to-end — it has NO explicit IOI or tempo feature, yet still places tuplets better than us, so the gap is likely our SSL not learning the IOI->triplet mapping robustly. Make IOI explicit: add per-note local IOI (onset[i]-onset[i-1]) and a short rolling tempo/median-IOI estimate as extra input embeddings. Triplets manifest as IOI ratios near 1:1:1 filling a beat (3 equal IOIs summing to one beat) vs 1:1 for eighths; an explicit IOI feature plus the beat-phase from finding #1 makes 'three equal sub-beat IOIs => triplet' a near-linear cue. This is low-cost (input-feature only, no arch change) and complements the beat-grid signal; the piece-specific Bayesian work shows modeling local repetition/structure of IOIs is what disambiguates duration on a per-piece basis, which is our exact failure mode (0 tuplets on 9/14 pieces).

findings.technique: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) over a rhythm/density condition: randomly drop the conditioning during training so the model learns both conditional and unconditional distributions, then at inference amplify the conditional direction with a guidance scale to strengthen a weak attribute. Also: non-differentiable rule-guided control over note-density / rhythm-complexity attributes.
findings.source: Conditional Diffusion as Latent Constraints / CFG attribute control for symbolic music, 2024-2025 (arXiv:2511.07156, arXiv:2402.14285) and the Controlling Rhythmic Complexity thesis (Utrecht). https://arxiv.org/html/2511.07156 ; https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14285v4
findings.how_to_apply: Once you add a tuplet-presence/density condition (finding #2 or #3), CFG lets you turn it UP at inference without retraining-time over-forcing that broke placement. During training, drop the per-bar tuplet/density condition with prob ~0.1-0.2 to get an unconditional pass; at inference compute logits both with and without the (predicted) condition and use logit_cond + w*(logit_cond - logit_uncond) with a modest w. This selectively boosts tuplet emission ONLY in bars where the predicted condition says tuplets belong, addressing under-production without the global-count side effects. Caveat/skeptical note: CFG is native to diffusion/AR LMs; in an enc-dec it works on the decoder logits and needs the condition to be in the decoder's conditioning path — pair it with finding #2's per-bar tokens. Treat as a second-stage amplifier after you've verified the per-bar condition gives an oracle improvement, not a first move.

findings.technique: Instantaneous Information Content (IIC) curve matching for steering local complexity. Define a per-position surprisal proxy from the model's own probabilities; IIC empirically correlates with rhythmic complexity and note density; use beam search to make generated output track a target IIC curve.
findings.source: Controlling Surprisal in Music Generation via Information Content Curve Matching, 2024 (arXiv:2408.06022, Bjare/Lattner/Widmer). https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06022
findings.how_to_apply: Inference-time only, no retraining: at decode, bias beam search toward hypotheses whose local IIC (or a direct surrogate: count of /3-grid durations per bar) matches a target complexity curve estimated from the performance MIDI's local IOI-irregularity. Practically, since IIC correlates with rhythmic complexity, you can drive the existing decoder with a target 'tuplet-density curve' derived from where the performance shows triplet-like IOI patterns, and use constrained/weighted beam search to prefer tuplet durations there. Lower relevance because it's a softer, heuristic decode-time lever and IIC correlation with rhythm weakens with context length; best used as a cheap diagnostic/decode-time nudge to confirm the model CAN place tuplets when biased, before committing to the training-time conditioning in findings #1-#3.

top_techniques.technique: Beat-relative output tokenization: replace the absolute measure-relative 1/24 offset stream with (beat-index, within-beat-subdivision) output heads. Confirmed against code: tokenizer.py PARAMS encodes offset/duration/downbeat all as absolute 1/24-step buckets (lines 38-40), so a triplet at phase 1/3 is a rare absolute bucket the model rarely commits to. Re-express onset/duration RELATIVE to the enclosing beat (24 sub-beats keeps both straight multiples-of-6 and triplet multiples-of-8 representable).
top_techniques.why: This is the single highest-leverage change. It converts the global, expressive-timing-destroyed question 'is this a triplet?' into a LOCAL per-beat subdivision choice, which is where placement actually lives. Directly attacks the NoteDuration sub-stream and fixes placement (right pieces) because the grid is per-note, not a global rate. Wachter beats Beyer's absolute grid on MUSTER onset error (12.30 vs ~12.69) using exactly this. Must be an OUTPUT-representation change at pretraining time, not a conditioning token appended to the absolute-grid model -- which is precisely why your prior input-side beat-conditioning fine-tune was neutral-to-destructive.
top_techniques.source: Wachter, Murgul & Heizmann, 'Beat-Based Rhythm Quantization of MIDI Performances', AES AIML-Audio 2025 (arXiv:2508.19262); companion arXiv:2604.22290; builds on Beyer & Dai ISMIR 2024 (arXiv:2410.00210). Verified gap in code: tokenizer.py lines 38-40 use absolute 1/24 offset, no beat-relative head.
top_techniques.effort: High (tokenizer refactor + new beat-index/subdivision heads + SSL retrain). Highest leverage; requires the non-drifting SSL-retrain vehicle, not a fine-tune of the released ckpt.

top_techniques.technique: Metrical-position-conditioned duration logit bias: precompute an empirical P(note_value | sub_beat_position) table from the engraved-score corpus and add log P as an additive offset to the decoder's NoteDuration logits. Makes triplet durations cheap only at sub-beats {0,8,16} and expensive at binary positions {6,12,18}.
top_techniques.why: A position-conditioned logit offset (not a flat tuplet upweight) stops the model scattering tuplets onto wrong/binary positions -- it fixes PLACEMENT where bucket-reweighting only fixed COUNT. Cheap lookup-table offset, fully compatible with the existing RoFormer decoder and 1/24 grid (map sub-beats to metrical position b_n). Pairs cleanly with grammar decoding: prior shapes likelihood, grammar enforces legality.
top_techniques.source: Shibata, Nakamura & Yoshii, 'Non-Local Musical Statistics as Guides for Audio-to-Score Piano Transcription', Information Sciences 566 (2021), arXiv:2008.12710 (L_NV = sum ln P(r_n | b_n); tatum = 1/3 of a 16th note).
top_techniques.effort: Low (precompute table + additive logit offset on duration head; can be applied at inference first with zero retrain). Best fast lever to validate the placement hypothesis.

top_techniques.technique: Logit adjustment on the NoteDuration sub-vocabulary: post-hoc argmax_y[f_y(x) - tau*log(pi_y)] at inference, or the Fisher-consistent train-time loss log[1 + sum (pi_y'/pi_y)^tau exp(f_y' - f_y)]. Apply ONLY to duration tokens, with pi estimated over the 1/24-grid duration vocabulary.
top_techniques.why: Principled replacement for the loss-reweighting you already tried and which failed. Menon proves naive 1/pi reweighting is NOT Fisher-consistent in separable regimes and over-emits; logit adjustment shifts the DECISION BOUNDARY by an additive per-token offset, flipping a prediction to a tuplet value only when evidence already nearly supports it. Pieces with no triplet evidence keep low triplet logits -> placement preserved, unlike resampling. The inference-time tau also gives you a reversible per-piece global tuplet dial for product use (train-prior/test-prior swap).
top_techniques.source: Menon et al., 'Long-tail learning via logit adjustment', ICLR 2021 (arXiv:2007.07314).
top_techniques.effort: Very low (post-hoc tau-sweep in [0.5,1.5] on duration logits, zero retrain). Run this first as a calibration probe before any fine-tune.

top_techniques.technique: Grammar / weighted-tree-automaton constrained decoding: a small CFG over each beat's sub-tatums (binary chain 24->12->6->3 vs ternary 24->8->4) with per-rule weights (triplet costlier than duplet); grammar-masked beam search or top-k beat-level rhythm rerank by model_logprob + lambda*grammar_weight.
top_techniques.why: Structural cure for the two placement failure modes global knobs cannot touch: 'lone/incomplete triplet' and 'triplet on the wrong piece'. The 1-best parse GUARANTEES no illegal/incomplete tuplet is emitted, and per-rule weights tune triplet propensity globally without retraining. Per-beat and timing-driven, so it adds tuplets only on beats whose performed onsets cluster at 0.33/0.67 phase and leaves straight beats alone.
top_techniques.source: Foscarin, Jacquemard, Rigaux & Sakai, 'A Parse-Based Framework for Coupled Rhythm Quantization and Score Structuring', MCM 2019 (hal-01988990); qparse. Decode-time rescorer variant: enumerate beat subdivisions {2,3,4,6}, pick argmax of sum log-prob(snapped durations) - lambda*complexity(n).
top_techniques.effort: Medium (decode-time reranker/mask over existing logits; no retrain to start). Pairs with the metrical-position prior; tune lambda on the 14-piece ASAP set.

top_techniques.technique: MiLe (entropy-difficulty) reweighting on the duration head: L = -(1 - H(p))^gamma * log(p_target), with H(p) the entropy of the model's duration distribution at that step.
top_techniques.why: Naive focal loss keys off only the gold-token probability and mislabels genuinely ambiguous binary-vs-triplet positions as 'hard', over-amplifying -- a plausible mechanism for how your knobs pushed tuplets onto wrong pieces. MiLe weights by the ENTROPY of the whole duration distribution, up-weighting the true tuplet-vs-binary decision points and leaving confident binary steps alone. Purely local/context-dependent, needs no frequency stats or resampling, so it cannot steer the corpus toward wrong pieces.
top_techniques.source: Su et al., 'MiLe Loss', NAACL Findings 2024 (arXiv:2310.19531).
top_techniques.effort: Low-medium (drop-in CE multiplier on the duration head during a short low-LR fine-tune). Sweep gamma in [0.5,2].

top_techniques.technique: Per-bar/per-beat tuplet-presence + density conditioning, derived for FREE from the score target during masked-SSL, with a gradient-detached auxiliary head that predicts it from the performance MIDI at inference (FIGARO-style expert features + difficulty-aware auxiliary supervision).
top_techniques.why: A LOCAL control that says WHICH bars carry tuplets -- exactly what global reweighting/resampling could not specify. Auto-derivable from the SSL target (does this bar use the /3 part of the grid?), so it is free supervision. The auxiliary head (beta~0.1, gradient-detached) is the documented fix for why a lone conditioning token is weak (69% -> 93% control), and forces internal reps to encode density so conditioning steers PLACEMENT not just COUNT. Feed numeric descriptors (IOI, displacement rate) not just an easy/hard label.
top_techniques.source: FIGARO (ICLR 2023, arXiv:2201.10936); 'Difficulty-Aware Score Generation for Piano Sight-Reading' 2025 (arXiv:2509.16913).
top_techniques.effort: Medium (auxiliary head + per-bar feature derivation + self-conditioning inference path). This is the principled, evidence-backed form of E3.

plan.maps_to_our_experiments:
E1 (output-prior rebalance via more tuplet-rich unpaired corpus) -- MODIFY/DEMOTE. The literature is unanimous that adding tuplet-rich corpus is a GLOBAL marginal shift, the same family as your already-failed bucket-reweighting and rate-resampling (Menon: not Fisher-consistent, over-emits; the reweighting-vs-conditioning synthesis: global priors raise count and necessarily misplace). Keep only a constrained version: oversample at the MEASURE level so the decoder sees tuplet durations IN-CONTEXT (Beyer ablation: augmentation crucial, beat-level alignment), and keep tempo/onset jitter SMALL (Beyer 0.8-1.2 tempo / sd 0.05 onset) because aggressive tempo scaling blurs the 0.33/0.67 phase cue and hurts downbeat accuracy. Do not expect E1 alone to fix placement.

E2 (denoising-SSL: corrupt surrogate timing, reconstruct clean rhythm) -- REINFORCE, and make it the vehicle for the beat-relative retokenization (Technique 1). Your masked-SSL already corrupts timing; the key modification is that the SSL TARGET must retain the score's tuplet phase (durations on the /3 grid) and the targets should be BEAT-RELATIVE, so denoising teaches 'three equal sub-beat IOIs -> triplet' as a local reconstruction. This is the non-drifting retrain vehicle your memory says you need (released-ckpt fine-tunes drift MUSTER). Add the metrical-position prior (Technique 2) and the per-bar tuplet-presence aux signal (Technique 6) into this stage.

E3 (per-piece tuplet-density conditioning token) -- MODIFY substantially. A single per-piece token is the weak-conditioning failure mode (69% control). Upgrade to: (a) PER-BAR not per-piece granularity, (b) numeric IOI/displacement descriptors not a categorical label, (c) a gradient-detached auxiliary head predicting tuplet-presence/density (raises control to ~93%), (d) free SSL supervision by deriving the feature from the target, with a self-conditioning classifier at inference. Optionally add classifier-free guidance (drop the condition with p~0.1-0.2 in training; amplify at inference) only AFTER an oracle/teacher-forced run confirms the per-bar condition gives a real ceiling lift. Note your memory's warning: do NOT bolt beat/density conditioning onto the absolute-grid released ckpt -- it must reshape tokenization/training.

E4 (duration-stream/focal/scheduled tuplet loss) -- REINFORCE but REPLACE plain focal loss. Sequence the loss work as: (1) post-hoc logit-adjustment tau-sweep on duration logits (zero retrain, Technique 3) to test whether placement is a calibration problem at all; (2) MiLe entropy-difficulty loss on the duration head (Technique 5) -- the correct entropy-keyed alternative to focal, which keys off only the gold token and mislabels ambiguous positions; (3) token-level adaptive training with floored, expectation-bounded weights (w>=1, weighted-mean in [1,1+delta], chi-square form for the extreme tail) as a short low-LR fine-tune that up-weights tuplet durations WITHOUT down-weighting the common binary durations that dominate the 9 zero-tuplet pieces (Gu et al. EMNLP 2020, arXiv:2010.04380). Keep delta<=~0.3 to bound MUSTER drift on other sub-streams.
novel_ideas: Self-similarity / repeat-consistency rerank (not in queue): after a first decode pass, compute a note-value self-similarity matrix over beats and rerank so highly-similar (repeated) beats receive the SAME subdivision. Directly cures the 'right rhythm, wrong bar' symptom your global knobs created by enforcing within-piece rhythmic self-consistency, turning one correct tuplet read into many correctly-placed ones (grounded in Shibata/Nakamura/Yoshii 2021 self-similarity priors). Cheap decode-time version first; train-time contrastive variant in SSL if it pays off.

Two-head detection/emission decoupling (not in queue): add an auxiliary per-note binary 'is-tuplet-member' head (denominator divisible by 3 on the 1/24 grid), trained class-balanced/logit-adjusted, conditioned on encoder features that SEE real performance micro-timing. At decode, gate tuplet-valued duration tokens by this calibrated detector above a swept threshold. Converts the entangled count/placement softmax into a calibrated detection threshold you can tune on the MUSTER NoteDuration precision/recall curve (analogous to two-stage objectness-then-class decoupling).

Inference-time tau as a product-facing tuplet dial (not in queue): expose the logit-adjustment tau on duration logits as a per-piece inference knob (train-prior->test-prior swap), giving a reversible global tuplet-rate control that leaves no-tuplet pieces untouched -- a safer product lever than any data/loss change.

Rare-duration embedding anisotropy fix (not in queue, run only if calibration plateaus): adaptive gradient gating (AGG) or frequency-aware contrastive learning on the duration OUTPUT embeddings (1/3,1/6,1/12,...) so rare-duration vectors keep larger, separable norms. Addresses placement at the REPRESENTATION level -- the hypothesis that the model can emit the token but cannot place it because rare-duration embeddings collapse into a narrow cone (Yu et al. ACL 2022; Zhang et al. AAAI 2022).

Lightweight tuplet-grouping head (not in queue, heaviest): predict per-beat groupwise 'these k notes form one tuplet' labels from engraved-score tuplet brackets (music21 exposes tuplet objects), used to gate the grammar mask or as an SSL aux loss. Makes ternary subdivision an explicit grouping decision over co-located notes -- the structurally-correct cure for 0-tuplets-on-9/14 -- but only worth it if beat-relative grid + metrical prior + grammar do not already close the NoteDuration gap (EngravingGNN arXiv:2509.19412; Foscarin graph decoder arXiv:2306.16955).

Use a strong external beat tracker to DEFINE the grid for the grammar rescorer (not in queue), NOT as decoder conditioning: PM2S / the 2025 transformer MIDI beat tracker (arXiv:2507.00466, ~97-98% beat F1) supplies beat boundaries on unlabeled classical SSL data so beat-relative targets and the per-beat subdivision search are available beyond the 822 ASAP pairs. Respects your logged lesson that beats-as-input-features were neutral; beats help at the tokenization/rescoring/grid stage.
plan.caveats:
Recommended sequencing (cheapest placement-true lever first): (1) post-hoc logit-adjustment tau-sweep on duration logits [zero retrain] -> (2) metrical-position-conditioned logit bias [precompute table, zero/low retrain] -> (3) grammar/qparse-style per-beat rescorer [decode-time] -> (4) MiLe duration-head + token-adaptive floored loss [short low-LR fine-tune] -> (5) beat-relative retokenization in the denoising-SSL stage [full retrain, highest leverage] -> only then detector head / anisotropy / grouping head if placement still misses.

Hard constraints from your own logs/memory: (a) Do NOT fine-tune the released ckpt for any structural change -- it drifts MUSTER; use the SSL-retrain vehicle (data-scaling-derisk, ar-from-scratch memories). (b) Do NOT re-run beat info as a decoder/encoder INPUT conditioning token on the absolute-grid model -- your beat-conditioning fine-tune was neutral-to-destructive and the gold-beat ceiling was flat; beat info must reshape TOKENIZATION at pretraining time. (c) E1 (more tuplet-rich corpus) is the same global-prior family that already failed for placement -- demote it.

Method caveats: logit adjustment is provably weak in SEPARABLE regimes -- if your duration model is already well-separated on tuplet tokens, tau will have little effect; that is itself diagnostic. MiLe can slightly raise overall perplexity while improving medium/hard tokens -- acceptable since MUSTER NoteDuration ER is the target, not likelihood. Token-adaptive weights need the weighted-mean bound (delta<=~0.3) or you will drift other sub-streams. CFG is native to diffusion/AR LMs; in an enc-dec it must operate on decoder logits with the condition in the decoder path, and only after an oracle run confirms the per-bar condition lifts the ceiling. The anisotropy and grouping-head ideas were validated on NMT/word embeddings and GNNs respectively, not rhythm grids -- treat as later-stage experiments, not first moves. Grammar/self-similarity reranks need reliable beat boundaries; pretty_midi fallback beats are weaker than PM2S, so phase clustering (the triplet vs straight cue) will be noisier -- validate the rescorer with gold ASAP beats first, then swap in a strong tracker. Finally, watch the count/placement tradeoff on ALL 14 pieces, not aggregate ER: the prior failures recovered count while misplacing, so report per-piece tuplet precision/recall, not just total count or mean MUSTER.
